parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Martin Kellogg of Newington, Connecticut
Capt. Martin Kellogg of Newington, Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: He kept the Hooker Elm's famous hospitality alive
narrative_blocks.body: He inherited the Farmington estate and the famous Hooker Elm from his father John. EH described him as 'a thorough gentleman of the old school' who continued the famed hospitality of the elm gatherings.

narrative_blocks.header: Favorite son and keeper of family history
narrative_blocks.body: He accompanied his father John on official journeys around the Colony and brought his first wife to live in the family house. EH wrote that he received from his father full information about the family's English origins — and that his father was the best informed of all Thomas Hooker's grandsons on the subject.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sisters married Hart brothers — knot tied
narrative_blocks.body: His sisters Abigail, Mary, and Sarah each married a son of Capt. John Hart between 1719 and 1725. Through those three marriages, Roger found himself simultaneously brother-in-law to Nathaniel, Samuel, and Matthew Hart — the Hart and Hooker families fully intertwined.

narrative_blocks.header: His granddaughter Abigail married Samuel Talcott
narrative_blocks.body: His son Thomas Hooker fathered Abigail Hooker, who married Samuel Talcott of Hartford. The marriage merged the Hooker surname into the Talcott line — a transition that defines several gen 6+ branches.

bio.notes: Named for maternal grandfather Capt. Martin Kellogg

narrative_blocks.header: He and Roger bought 40 acres from Tunxis
narrative_blocks.body: In 1776, he and his brother Roger purchased 40 acres at Congee, Farmington, including a dwelling house and barn, from Tunxis men Cornelius Indian and Elijah Wampey for £150. The deed is among the documented transfers of Native land in the Farmington area, preserved at the Native Northeast Portal.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Georgian Colonial; family home for three Hooker generations.

cross_connections.display_label: First cousins who served together — Roger enlisted in Noadiah's company.
First cousins once removed — both married into the Treadwell family.

notable.notable_blurb: Continental Army Major; Society of the Cincinnati

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of the Society of the Cincinnati — Revolutionary War officers' hereditary society.

narrative_blocks.header: First from Farmington to Boston — Noadiah led
narrative_blocks.body: At the Lexington alarm he volunteered in the company of his first cousin Capt. Noadiah Hooker — 111 men, the first company from Farmington and the first from Connecticut to reach the Continental Army at Boston. He entered as a private and rose through sergeant, ensign, and 2nd lieutenant to Major of the Continental Line.

narrative_blocks.header: Flag of truce — Burgoyne's surrender delivered
narrative_blocks.body: He was sent under a flag of truce to the British commander in New York to formally announce the surrender of Burgoyne's army in October 1777. He also helped fit out fire ships at New York in 1776 under Washington's plan to attack British vessels. EH records he was "throughout the war held in high esteem by Gen. Washington, and frequently entrusted with responsible and important duties."

narrative_blocks.header: West Indies mariner — then Sheriff until 70
narrative_blocks.body: Before the Revolution he made eleven voyages to the West Indies as a merchant mariner. After the war he became Sheriff of Hartford County, served as Justice of the Peace until age 70, and was a Farmington selectman. He disposed of his estate to his brother-in-law before his death, retaining possession during his life.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife was the Governor's sister, not daughter
narrative_blocks.body: EH called Mary Treadwell the "daughter" of Gov. John Treadwell — impossible, as Treadwell was born 1745 and Mary in 1755, making him only ten at her birth. Ephraim Treadwell's 1781 will names both "daughter Mary" and "son John Treadwell" as executor, confirming they were siblings.

narrative_blocks.header: Purchased Tunxis land with Elnathan in 1776
narrative_blocks.body: In 1776, he and his brother Elnathan purchased 40 acres at Congee, Farmington — including a dwelling house and barn — from Tunxis men Cornelius Indian and Elijah Wampey for £150. The deed is preserved at the Native Northeast Portal as one of the documented transfers of Native land in the Farmington area.

narrative_blocks.header: Cincinnati member — half-brother Thomas died in war
narrative_blocks.body: He and his brother-in-law Capt. Simeon Newell were both original members of the Society of the Cincinnati, founded 1783 by Continental officers. His half-brother Thomas Hart Hooker (different mother, Mercy Hart) died of pleurisy during the same Revolution — at Roxbury in 1775, age 30 — never seeing its end.

military_service.unit: Company of Capt. Noadiah Hooker; Continental Line (eventually Major)
military_service.notes: Enlisted at first call (Lexington alarm) in company of his cousin Capt. Noadiah Hooker — 111 men, first from Farmington and first from Connecticut to reach the army at Boston. Rose: Private → Sergeant → Ensign → 2nd Lieutenant (under Washington) → Major. 1776: Fitted out fire ships at New York to attack British vessels (Washington's plan). As brigade major: managed correspondence for generals on whose staff he served. Sent under flag of truce to British commander in New York to announce Burgoyne's surrender. "Throughout the war held in high esteem by Gen. Washington, and frequently entrusted with responsible and important duties." Member, Society of the Cincinnati.

narrative_blocks.header: She was the Governor's sister
narrative_blocks.body: EH entry 306 called her "daughter of Gov. John Treadwell" — but Treadwell was born in 1745, making him ten years old at her 1755 birth. Both were children of Ephraim Treadwell, confirmed by his 1781 will naming "daughter Mary" and "son John Treadwell" as executor.

burial.plot_notes: Saint James Episcopal Church Memorial Garden, Farmington CT

narrative_blocks.header: Husband and half-brother both Cincinnati members
narrative_blocks.body: Her husband Capt. Simeon Newell and her half-brother Maj. Roger Hooker both served as Continental officers and were original members of the Society of the Cincinnati. After the war, both families moved from Connecticut to western New York — Simeon dying at Sodus Bay in 1813, Mercy following in 1817 at Wolcott.

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of the Society of the Cincinnati — Revolutionary War officers' hereditary society.

education.notes: — Yale 1775. Captain, Continental Army. Society of the Cincinnati. Husband of Mercy Hooker (H00307, Hooker descendant).

narrative_blocks.header: Martin died young
narrative_blocks.body: Martin died in 1785, aged about one.

naming_inspiration.notes: Named for Hon. Roger Sherman of New Haven, signer of the Declaration of Independence and the Constitution. Per existing research_notes, 'a close' relation — specific kinship requires further research. Newell's parents are X00258 (unnamed) and H00307 Mercy Hooker Newell.

narrative_blocks.header: Named for Hon. Roger Sherman, Constitution signer
narrative_blocks.body: Roger Sherman Newell was born in 1791 in Southington CT, less than two years before his namesake Hon. Roger Sherman died in 1793. The Newell family clearly traveled in the Sherman family's orbit — Roger Sherman of New Haven was the only Founding Father to sign all four great state papers, and the choice to give an infant the full 'Roger Sherman' as first and middle name was a strong family memorial.

narrative_blocks.header: Dolly died young
narrative_blocks.body: Dolly died in 1794, aged about one. Dolly’s parents gave the name to a child born two years later.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Isaac Cowles

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. Solomon Cowles of Farmington CT

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary lieutenant-colonel; Farmington farmer and tavern-keeper

military_service.pension: File No. 9153, U.S. Pension Office. Pensioned.
military_service.notes: Served 4th Sgt, 1st Co., Col. Hooker's 15th CT Militia, May 1776-May 1785. Jul 1776: went with company to New York under Capt. John Porter; Battle of Long Island; returned to Kingsbridge. Appointed quartermaster-sergeant by QM Jonathan Bull; served to close of the war. At Danbury Alarm; at New Haven (BTL018); at Battle of Saratoga (BTL011). Pensioned. Pension File No. 9153.

narrative_blocks.header: He fought from Long Island to Saratoga
narrative_blocks.body: Isaac Cowles rose from sergeant to lieutenant-colonel commandant of the 15th Connecticut Militia during the Revolution. He saw action at the Battle of Long Island, the Danbury and New Haven alarms, and Saratoga.

narrative_blocks.header: Afterward he kept a Farmington tavern
narrative_blocks.body: Back home in Farmington, Cowles farmed his father's homestead and kept a tavern, prospering into a man of local standing. A federal pension file recorded his long Revolutionary service.

narrative_blocks.header: Enrolled Yale at 15
narrative_blocks.body: In 1797, scarcely fifteen years old, Horace Cowles was admitted to the freshman class at Yale College. A growing disability forced him to leave in his senior year before examinations; invited by the faculty to attend with his class, he declined and was never enrolled as a graduate. He became a merchant with his brother, then justice of the peace, selectman, town clerk, state legislator, choir instructor, and member of the Missionary, Temperance, Anti-Masonic, and Anti-Slavery Societies.

narrative_blocks.header: One of three Mende girls from the Amistad
narrative_blocks.body: After the Amistad captives were freed, the Mende from that ship spent eight months in Farmington while arrangements were made for their return to Sierra Leone. Horace Cowles took in one of the three young Mende girls — believed to be Teme, also called Tamie — who attended school with the other Mende each morning and learned domestic skills with the Cowles children. Horace fell ill toward the end of the Mende's stay and died in 1841; his son Samuel Smith inherited the house and continued the Underground Railroad work.

open_questions.question: Is Rev. Noah Porter Sr. (1781-1866), who preached Horace Cowles's funeral sermon, a relation to the Hooker tree?
open_questions.notes: Noah Porter Sr. was minister of the First Church of Christ in Farmington 1806-1866 (60 years). He preached Horace Cowles's funeral sermon, published as a 26-page pamphlet. His son Noah Porter Jr. (1811-1892) became president of Yale. The Porter family is prominent in Farmington history — worth checking whether there is a Hooker or Cowles family connection. (Cowles Genealogy #642)

cross_connections.display_label: Rev. Noah Porter preached his funeral sermon — 26-page pamphlet, Farmington 1841
Willcox held the Mende captives in federal custody during their trial; Cowles housed one of the Mende girls in his Farmington home after the Supreme Court freed them in 1841.
Both Cowles cousins sheltered Mende girls in 1841
cross_connections.description: When the freed Amistad Mende were brought to Farmington in 1841, Horace Cowles and his cousin Major Timothy Cowles each took in one of the three surviving Mende girls. Horace sheltered Teme; Timothy is believed to have sheltered Kagne.

cross_connections.display_label: Fellow Farmington abolitionist and Underground Railroad partner.
Hooker-descendant Farmington abolitionists, Amistad-era neighbors.
Fellow Hooker-descendant Connecticut abolitionist of the same generation.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: Underground Railroad station; Amistad Mende girl stayed here.
Underground Railroad station; Amistad Mende girl stayed here.

education.institution_blurb: Admitted 1797 age 15; left in senior year due to disability; never formally graduated despite faculty invitation.

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909 — entry #698 Major Timothy Cowles

notable.notable_blurb: Wealthy Farmington merchant who sheltered a Mende child

narrative_blocks.header: Built Farmington's stone store — and ran it
narrative_blocks.body: Major Timothy Cowles was a wealthy Farmington merchant whose home sat just south of the Congregational Church. He built the stone store destroyed by fire in 1864, owned the large hotel building later occupied by Porter Seminary, and served as state senator (1835), member of the Yale Corporation (1835) and liberal contributor to Yale's $100,000 fund, and president of Farmington Bank (1852). His estate at death was inventoried at $147,634.

narrative_blocks.header: Sheltered an Amistad survivor
narrative_blocks.body: When the three Mende girls who had survived the 1839 Amistad rebellion were brought to Farmington in 1841, the Cowles family took in one of them — believed by NPS to be Kagne. She would have attended school five hours each morning and shared in the family's domestic routines.

narrative_blocks.header: Gave liberally to Yale's Hundred Thousand Fund
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy Cowles graduated Yale and later served as a 'liberal contributor' to the Hundred Thousand Dollar Fund raised by Yale alumni in support of the college. He earned the title 'Major Timothy' from Connecticut militia service that followed him into family records.

institutions.institution_blurb: Member of the Yale Corporation 1835; contributed to Yale's $100,000 fund.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Cowles cousins sheltered Mende girls in 1841
cross_connections.description: When the freed Amistad Mende were brought to Farmington in 1841, Timothy Cowles and his cousin Horace Cowles each took in one of the three surviving Mende girls. Timothy is believed to have sheltered Kagne; Horace sheltered Teme.

cross_connections.display_label: Both Farmington households that sheltered Mende girls in 1841
cross_connections.description: During the Amistad survivors' 1841 Farmington stay, Rev. Noah Porter Jr.'s family took in Margru while Timothy Cowles sheltered the girl believed to be Kagne. Both families were anchors of the Farmington abolitionist network that received the freed Mende.

cross_connections.display_label: Williams brought the Mende to Farmington — Cowles took one in
cross_connections.description: Austin Williams was one of the Farmington abolitionists who organized the freed Mende's relocation to Farmington after the 1841 Supreme Court ruling. Timothy Cowles was among the families who answered Williams's call by sheltering a Mende child.

cross_connections.display_label: Held the Mende during trial — Cowles housed one after release
cross_connections.description: Norris Willcox held the Mende captives in federal custody during their trial. After the Supreme Court freed them in March 1841, the Mende were brought to Farmington, where Major Timothy Cowles was among the families who sheltered one of the freed Mende girls.

cross_connections.display_label: Married his fourth cousin — both descend Rev. Samuel Hooker.

military_service.notes: Quartermaster 1808-9; ensign 1809; lt. Grenadier co. 1811; captain 1812-14; major 1815; major 14th CT Militia 1816-17.
Got the title 'Major Timothy' through service in the Connecticut militia. The title stuck in family records and EH 1909.

career.notes: Liberal contributor to the $100,000 fund for the assistance of the college.

career.title: Merchant and farmer
career.notes: Merchant and farmer at Farmington CT per EH #698.

career.title: Connecticut State Senator
career.notes: Served as State Senator per EH #698.

education.notes: Yale graduate per EH #698. Specific year not documented in EH text.

military_service.notes: Lt. 15th CT Militia 1815; lt. 14th CT Militia 1816-17; ensign 1817-18; lt. 1819-20; captain 1821-24.

narrative_blocks.header: Solomon died young
narrative_blocks.body: Solomon died in 1795 at the age of one. Solomon’s parents gave the name to a child born one year later.

narrative_blocks.header: His homestead stood on the site of Rev.
narrative_blocks.body: Solomon Cowles lived on his father's homestead in Farmington — the house built upon the site of the original parsonage occupied by Rev. Samuel Hooker when he was minister at Farmington. He was a farmer and manufacturer of soap and candles, joined the church in 1834. His wife Mary Emeline Sloper was buried beside him at Farmington.

cross_connections.display_label: His homestead stood on the site of Rev. Samuel Hooker's original parsonage

naming_inspiration.description: Named 'Samuel Hooker' to honor the Hooker family lineage through his mother Lucina Hooker Cowles (H00309) and the family's descent from Rev. Thomas Hooker.

institution_affiliations.institution_blurb: Graduated Andover Theological Seminary 1824 but never preached.

education.institution_blurb: Graduated M.A. Yale 1821. See Biographical Notices of Yale College Graduates.
Graduated Andover Theological Seminary 1824 but never preached.

burial.plot_notes: Bolton Center Cemetery, Bolton, Tolland County, Connecticut. Cenotaphic inscription also on Olcott family monument in West Cemetery, Manchester CT.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a Yale-educated 'quaint and erratic' preacher
narrative_blocks.body: Cynthia married Rev. Allen Olcott in 1792 — a Yale College 1768 graduate settled at the Farmington Presbyterian Church in 1787. After four years his preaching was deemed 'quaint and erratic' enough that he was dismissed and never settled at any other church.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled with Allen on a South Manchester farm
narrative_blocks.body: After his Farmington dismissal Allen returned to his native East Hartford and then settled with Cynthia on a farm at South Manchester, Connecticut. Family stories suggest his farming methods were as unique as his preaching had been. He died there in April 1811, leaving Cynthia a widow with five children ranging from eight to eighteen.

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried Capt. Samuel Alvord at fifty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Two years after Allen's death Cynthia remarried — Capt. Samuel Alvord of East Hartford, on 14 January 1813. The couple lived at East Hartford until her own death in June 1827. Through her first marriage's daughter Cynthia, her line married back into the Talcott-Hooker convergence.

parents.father_research_notes: Captain Josiah Olcott of East Hartford CT. Not built — does not auto-qualify under Jalapeño Rule. Per EH 1909 entry 310 narrative.
parents.mother_research_notes: Penelope Beckwith Olcott, wife of Capt. Josiah Olcott of East Hartford CT. Not built — does not auto-qualify under Jalapeño Rule. Per EH 1909 entry 310 narrative.

H00704.prominent_name_reason: Charles Olcott (1793-1857) — pioneer Medina Ohio lawyer, abolitionist author, and inventor of the metallic (iron) ship (patented 1835). His book "A Blow at Slavery" was read aloud in Congress. Great-great-great-great-grandchild of Rev. Thomas Hooker.

cross_connections.display_label: Charles Olcott and Betsey Mix Cowles were Hooker descendants active.

notable.notable_blurb: Abolitionist whose lectures were read in Congress

narrative_blocks.header: His abolitionist lectures were read aloud in Congress
narrative_blocks.body: His 1838 'Two Lectures on Slavery and Abolition' was read aloud in Congress as evidence for the unconstitutional nature of slavery. Edward Hooker called him 'a clear, bold writer, and a great Anti-Slavery man.'

narrative_blocks.header: Patented iron ship 1835; Navy rejected as impractical
narrative_blocks.body: On July 13, 1835 he patented an iron ship — 'a new and useful improvement in ship building and naval architecture, consisting in constructing ships wholly of iron.' The Naval board rejected it as 'entirely impractical,' but the Navy built iron ships shortly after; he never received compensation.

narrative_blocks.header: Mentored Joel Tiffany — refrigerator car inventor
narrative_blocks.body: He mentored Joel Tiffany, a Connecticut-born law student who later invented the refrigerator railroad car and authored abolitionist legal theory. Both Olcott and Tiffany were part of Medina's network of Connecticut-rooted reformers in the 1830s-50s.

education.notes: — Yale 1816. EH incorrectly states 1805 (impossible — born 1793). Pioneer Medina Ohio lawyer, abolitionist, iron ship patent holder 1835. Son of Cynthia Hooker (H00310) and Rev. Allen Olcott (Yale 1768 — also in Hooker line circle). Two Yale graduates in the same Olcott family, father and son.

narrative_blocks.header: Manchester farmer and 'strong Anti-Slavery man'
narrative_blocks.body: Sidney farmed at Manchester Connecticut his entire adult life and was described in EH as a 'strong Anti-Slavery man.' He married Edna Alice Adams in 1824; she descended from William Bradford, second governor of Plymouth Colony. Their son James Bradford Olcott carried the Bradford name as a middle name forward — and the family abolition values into the next generation.

artworks.blurb: Co-stitched the surviving Olcott Sisters Samplers at Miss Mary Hart's School Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: Stitched a sampler at Miss Mary Hart's school
narrative_blocks.body: At nine years old in 1806, Anna stitched a classic 'house' sampler at Miss Mary Hart's School in Farmington Connecticut — the boarding school that preceded the famous Miss Porter's. The sampler bears the verse 'Let Virtue guide and God provide' and a family record naming herself and her four siblings. Her younger sister Cynthia made matching samplers in 1810 at the same school.

classification.lineage_note: Thomas¹ → Samuel² → John³ → Roger⁴ → Cynthia Hooker⁵(H00310) → Cynthia Olcott⁶

cross_connections.display_label: Mother-in-law was Hooker descendant from a separate line.
Father-in-law of the second Hooker-Talcott convergence.
Married her cousin Samuel descending through Roger Hooker

artworks.blurb: Co-stitched the surviving Olcott Sisters Samplers at Miss Mary Hart's School Farmington.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of a minister who married a Hooker
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Rev. Allen Olcott of Manchester, Connecticut had married Cynthia Hooker — pulling the Hooker bloodline into the Olcott family. The marriage made Cynthia herself a Hooker descendant from birth.

narrative_blocks.header: Joined two Hooker lines through her own marriage
narrative_blocks.body: She married Samuel Talcott in January 1822 — both bride and groom descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker through entirely separate lines, making this a second Hooker-Talcott convergence in the family. They had a son Samuel Olcott Talcott in December.

narrative_blocks.header: Died thirteen days after giving birth
narrative_blocks.body: She gave birth to son Samuel Olcott Talcott on December 19, 1822 and died at age 23 just over a month later, in West Hartford on January 23, 1823. Her son carried her family surname Olcott as his middle name.

cross_connections.display_label: Great-grandnephew Walter Hooker Olcott carries his name forward
